questions.questionText: A 55-year-old smoker presents with hemoptysis. Chest X-ray shows a central hilar mass with ipsilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Central lung masses in smokers — squamous cell and small cell are centrally located. Why correct: Smoker + hemoptysis + central hilar mass + hilar LN = squamous cell carcinoma (most common central type). Why A wrong: TB can cause hilar LN but presents with upper lobe cavitation and chronic history. Why C wrong: Adenocarcinoma is PERIPHERAL (most common overall lung cancer). Why D wrong: Hamartoma is benign, shows 'popcorn calcification,' peripheral. Clinical relevance: Central = squamous/small cell; Peripheral = adenocarcinoma/large cell. Exam trap: SCC is no longer the most common lung cancer overall — adenocarcinoma is (#1) — but SCC is the most common CENTRAL type. Memory aid: 'Squamous = Sentral (Central) + Smoker.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.

questions.questionText: A young man involved in a road traffic accident presents with respiratory distress. CXR shows opacification of left hemithorax with shift of mediastinum to the RIGHT. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Mediastinal shift TOWARDS opacity = collapse; AWAY from opacity = effusion/mass. Why correct: Opacified hemithorax + mediastinal shift to SAME side = collapse/atelectasis (volume loss pulls mediastinum toward it). Why A wrong: Massive effusion → mediastinal shift to OPPOSITE side (pushes away). Why C wrong: Pneumothorax → hyperlucent (not opacified) + mediastinal shift away. Why D wrong: Consolidation — air bronchograms, NO significant mediastinal shift (no volume change). Clinical relevance: Post-trauma — endobronchial blood clot causing lobar/lung collapse. Exam trap: The DIRECTION of mediastinal shift is the key differentiator — toward = collapse, away = effusion/tension pneumothorax. Memory aid: 'Collapse = Come towards; Effusion = Escape away.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.

questions.questionText: On chest X-ray, 'bat-wing' or 'butterfly' pattern of bilateral hilar opacities is characteristic of:
questions.optionB: Pulmonary edema (cardiogenic — bilateral perihilar alveolar opacities sparing periphery)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Bat-wing/butterfly opacities = bilateral perihilar alveolar edema. Why correct: Cardiogenic pulmonary edema — fluid accumulates in perihilar regions first → bilateral, symmetrical, 'bat-wing' opacities. Associated: cardiomegaly, upper lobe venous distension, Kerley B lines, pleural effusion. Why A wrong: Lymphoma — mediastinal lymphadenopathy, not butterfly opacification. Why C wrong: Sarcoidosis — bilateral hilar LYMPHADENOPATHY (BHL), not alveolar opacities. Why D wrong: Pulmonary hemorrhage can cause bilateral opacities but not typically bat-wing. Clinical relevance: CXR findings progress: cephalization → interstitial edema (Kerley B lines) → alveolar edema (bat-wing) → pleural effusion. Exam trap: Bat-wing can also be seen in pulmonary alveolar proteinosis and uremia — context matters. Memory aid: 'Bat-wing = Both sides, Around hilum, cardiogenic edema.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.

questions.questionText: A patient with sarcoidosis. CXR shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (BHL) with clear lung fields. This represents which stage?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Sarcoidosis CXR staging determines prognosis and management. Why correct: Stage I = BHL only (no pulmonary infiltrates) — best prognosis, >80% spontaneous resolution. Why A wrong: Stage 0 = normal CXR. Why C wrong: Stage II = BHL + pulmonary infiltrates. Why D wrong: Stage III = pulmonary infiltrates WITHOUT BHL. Stage IV = pulmonary fibrosis. Clinical relevance: Stage I often doesn't need treatment. BHL is bilateral and symmetric (asymmetric → think lymphoma). Exam trap: BHL WITHOUT parenchymal disease = Stage I (best prognosis). BHL WITH infiltrates = Stage II. Infiltrates WITHOUT BHL = Stage III (worse prognosis). Memory aid: 'Sarcoidosis: 0=Normal, I=hiLar, II=Lungs+hiLar, III=Lungs only, IV=Fibrosis.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.

questions.questionText: Kerley B lines on chest X-ray represent thickened:
questions.optionB: Interlobular septa (short, horizontal lines at lung bases perpendicular to pleural surface — seen in pulmonary edema and lymphangitis carcinomatosa)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Kerley B lines = thickened interlobular septa at lung bases. Why correct: Short (1-2cm), horizontal, linear opacities at costophrenic angles, perpendicular to pleural surface = edematous/thickened interlobular septa. Why A wrong: Thickened bronchial walls = peribronchial cuffing/tram-tracking (seen in bronchitis). Why C wrong: Fissures are linear but follow known anatomical planes (oblique, horizontal). Why D wrong: Pulmonary arteries are tubular, branching from hilum. Clinical relevance: Causes — pulmonary edema (most common, transient), lymphangitis carcinomatosa (permanent), sarcoidosis. Kerley A lines: longer, central, oblique. Exam trap: Kerley B lines = early sign of interstitial edema BEFORE alveolar flooding. Memory aid: 'Kerley B = Base of lung, Brief lines, perpendicular to pleural Border.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.
questions.conceptTag: Kerley B lines interlobular septa edema

questions.questionText: A 30-year-old man presents with acute chest pain and dyspnea. CXR shows a visible visceral pleural line with absent lung markings beyond it in the right apex. Diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Visible visceral pleural line + absent lung markings beyond = pneumothorax. Why correct: Air between visceral and parietal pleura → lung collapses → visible thin visceral pleural margin + hyperlucent area without vascular markings beyond. Why A wrong: Pneumomediastinum — air outlining mediastinal structures, not lung edge. Why C wrong: Pleural effusion — blunting of costophrenic angle, meniscus sign, opacification. Why D wrong: Bullous emphysema — thin-walled cystic space WITH some markings within bullae; no visceral pleural line. Clinical relevance: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax — tall, thin, young males. BTS guidelines: if rim >2cm on CXR = large pneumothorax. Exam trap: Expiratory CXR improves detection of small pneumothorax (lung becomes smaller, air space more visible). Skin folds can mimic — but skin folds have lung markings beyond. Memory aid: 'PneumoThorax = Pleural line Thin + Translucent beyond.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.

questions.questionText: CXR shows 'egg-on-string' appearance of the heart. This is most commonly seen in:
questions.optionB: Transposition of great arteries (TGA — narrow mediastinal pedicle due to superimposed great vessels, enlarged globular heart)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Egg-on-string = TGA. Why correct: TGA — aorta and PA are superimposed (parallel, not crossing) → narrow mediastinal pedicle ('string') + enlarged egg-shaped heart. Most common cyanotic heart disease presenting in NEONATES (first day). Why A wrong: TOF — boot-shaped heart (coeur en sabot), right aortic arch in 25%. Why C wrong: ASD — globular heart with increased pulmonary vascularity, no egg-on-string. Why D wrong: TAPVC — 'snowman/figure-of-eight' appearance (supracardiac type — dilated SVC, innominate vein, vertical vein). Clinical relevance: TGA needs PGE1 (keep PDA open) + balloon atrial septostomy (Rashkind) as bridge to switch operation. Exam trap: Boot-shaped = TOF, Egg-on-string = TGA, Snowman = TAPVC, Box-shaped = Ebstein anomaly. Memory aid: 'Egg needs Transporting = TGA = Egg-on-string.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.

questions.questionText: A 40-year-old male with cough and weight loss. CXR shows a thick-walled cavity in the right upper lobe with an air-fluid level. The most common cause of a cavity in the upper lobe is:
questions.explanation: Core concept: Upper lobe cavity — TB is the most common cause in endemic areas. Why correct: TB has predilection for upper lobes (higher oxygen tension). Cavitary TB: post-primary/reactivation TB, thick-walled cavity with surrounding consolidation/nodules. Why A wrong: Lung abscess — typically in dependent lobes (right lower lobe/posterior segments) with large air-fluid level. Why C wrong: SCC can cavitate (most common cavitating lung cancer) but TB is far more common in India. Why D wrong: GPA (Wegener's) — bilateral multiple cavitating nodules + c-ANCA. Clinical relevance: Upper lobe cavities = TB until proven otherwise in India. Lower lobe cavity = abscess (aspiration). Exam trap: Squamous cell carcinoma is the MOST COMMON CAVITATING CANCER (not overall cavity). TB is the MOST COMMON overall cavity in India. Memory aid: 'Upper lobe cavities = UCT (Upper = Chronic = TB).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.

questions.questionText: The 'golden S sign' (S sign of Golden) on CXR is seen in:
questions.optionB: Right upper lobe collapse due to a central obstructing mass (hilum pulled up + mass pushes minor fissure laterally → S-shaped contour)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Golden S sign = RUL collapse + central mass obstructing bronchus. Why correct: Central mass (usually bronchogenic carcinoma) obstructs RUL bronchus → RUL collapse (fissure pulled up medially) but mass prevents collapse at hilum (pushes fissure laterally at center) → S-shaped configuration of minor fissure. Why A wrong: Effusion — meniscus sign, not S sign. Why C wrong: RLL collapse — triangular opacity behind heart. Why D wrong: Pneumothorax — no fissural displacement. Clinical relevance: RUL collapse = elevated minor fissure, loss of right heart border silhouette if adjacent. LUL collapse = veil sign (hazy left hemithorax). Exam trap: Golden S sign = ALWAYS suspect underlying bronchogenic carcinoma. Memory aid: 'Golden S = Sinister (cancer) cause of RUL collapse.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.
questions.conceptTag: Golden S sign RUL collapse central mass

questions.optionB: Loss of normal cardiac/diaphragmatic silhouette when adjacent lung is opacified (disease in same plane as the structure)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Silhouette sign = loss of interface between structures of similar density. Why correct: When lung adjacent to heart border or diaphragm becomes opacified (consolidation/mass), the sharp border is lost because water-density tissue abuts water-density structure — no contrast. Why A wrong: Air bronchogram = air-filled bronchi visible within opacified lung (consolidation/atelectasis). Why C wrong: Visible pleural line = pneumothorax. Why D wrong: Mediastinal widening = aortic dissection/lymphoma. Clinical relevance: Right heart border lost = RML disease. Left heart border lost = lingula. Right hemidiaphragm lost = RLL. Left hemidiaphragm lost = LLL. Exam trap: Silhouette sign localizes the disease — it tells you WHICH lobe is involved. Memory aid: 'Silhouette = Same plane = Structure border Silently disappears.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.

questions.questionText: Eggshell calcification of hilar lymph nodes on CXR is most commonly associated with:
questions.explanation: Core concept: Eggshell calcification = peripheral rim calcification of lymph nodes = silicosis. Why correct: Silicosis — inhalation of silite/quartz dust. CXR: upper lobe predominant, small round opacities, progressive massive fibrosis (PMF), eggshell calcification of hilar/mediastinal LN (pathognomonic). Why A wrong: TB — calcified LN but usually dense/homogeneous, not eggshell. Why C wrong: Sarcoidosis rarely causes eggshell calcification (occasionally). Why D wrong: Lymphoma — enlarged nodes without calcification typically. Clinical relevance: Silicosis increases TB risk (silicotuberculosis). Also associated with RA (Caplan syndrome — rheumatoid pneumoconiosis). Exam trap: Eggshell calcification is also rarely seen in sarcoidosis, treated lymphoma, and amyloidosis — but silicosis is the CLASSIC and most tested association. Memory aid: 'Silicosis = Shell (eggshell) calcification.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.

questions.questionText: A 60-year-old woman with breast cancer shows a pleural effusion on CXR. What is the most reliable sign of a small pleural effusion on an erect PA CXR?
questions.optionB: Blunting of the costophrenic angle (meniscus sign — fluid level curves upward laterally)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Costophrenic angle blunting = earliest sign of pleural effusion on erect CXR. Why correct: Minimum ~200-300 mL needed to blunt costophrenic angle on erect PA CXR. Meniscus sign: concave upper surface curving higher laterally (fluid meniscus due to surface tension). Why A wrong: White-out = massive effusion (not 'small'). Why C wrong: Mediastinal shift occurs with large effusion only. Why D wrong: Elevated diaphragm = subpulmonic effusion (effusion mimicking diaphragm). Clinical relevance: Lateral decubitus film — detects as little as 50 mL (layers dependently). USG even more sensitive. Exam trap: Subpulmonary effusion can be missed — apparent elevated diaphragm + lateral shift of meniscus peak = clue. Memory aid: 'CPA Blunting = effusion Begins to appear (200mL).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.

questions.questionText: A patient with cystic fibrosis. CXR shows bilateral upper lobe bronchiectasis with a 'tramline' and 'ring shadow' pattern. What do tramlines represent?
questions.optionB: Thickened, dilated bronchial walls seen in parallel (longitudinal view of dilated bronchi)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Tramline shadows = dilated bronchi seen en face (longitudinal); Ring shadows = dilated bronchi seen end-on (cross-section). Why correct: In bronchiectasis, permanently dilated bronchi become visible as parallel linear shadows (tramlines when longitudinal) or ring shadows with signet-ring sign on CT (airway larger than adjacent vessel). Why A wrong: Pulmonary vessels taper centrally to peripherally; dilated bronchi maintain caliber or increase peripherally. Why C wrong: Lymphatic channels are not visible as tramlines. Why D wrong: Pleural thickening is peripheral, not airway-related. Clinical relevance: HRCT is gold standard for bronchiectasis (signet-ring sign). Causes: CF (#1 in Western), TB (#1 in India), ABPA, immunodeficiency. Exam trap: Tram-tracking can also be seen in chronic bronchitis (airway wall thickening without dilation). Memory aid: 'Tram-track = Two parallel Thick bronchial walls.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.

questions.questionText: On CXR, widening of the mediastinum (>8 cm) in a patient after a road traffic accident is most suggestive of:
questions.optionB: Traumatic aortic injury (rupture/transection — most commonly at the aortic isthmus, just distal to left subclavian artery)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Post-trauma widened mediastinum (>8cm at aortic knob) = suspect aortic injury. Why correct: Deceleration injury → aortic isthmus tear (tethered at ligamentum arteriosum). CXR signs: widened mediastinum, blurred aortic knob, deviation of NG tube/trachea to right, left hemothorax, apical cap. Why A wrong: Aortic dissection is spontaneous (hypertension), not trauma. Can also widen mediastinum. Why C wrong: Pericardial effusion — water-bottle shaped heart, not widened mediastinum. Why D wrong: Esophageal rupture (Boerhaave) — pneumomediastinum, not widened mediastinum. Clinical relevance: CT angiography confirms diagnosis. Most die at scene; those surviving to hospital need urgent repair. Exam trap: Widened mediastinum on SUPINE trauma CXR can be falsely positive (AP projection magnifies) — CT angiography needed for confirmation. Memory aid: 'Deceleration + Wide mediastinum = aortic Disruption.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.

questions.questionText: Miliary pattern on CXR (innumerable tiny 1-3mm nodules uniformly distributed) is most commonly caused by:
questions.explanation: Core concept: Miliary pattern = uniform tiny nodules resembling millet seeds. Why correct: Miliary TB — hematogenous dissemination of M. tuberculosis → uniform, tiny (1-3mm) nodules throughout both lungs. Immuno-compromised, children, elderly. Why A wrong: Sarcoidosis — perilymphatic distribution, upper and mid-zone, BHL. Can mimic but classic sarcoid is not truly miliary. Why C wrong: Metastases — usually variable-sized nodules (cannonball), lower lobe predominant. Why D wrong: Silicosis — upper zone predominant with PMF, rounded nodules larger than miliary. Clinical relevance: CXR may be NORMAL early in miliary TB — HRCT more sensitive. Fundoscopy: choroidal tubercles (pathognomonic). Exam trap: Miliary pattern DDx: TB (most common), fungal (histoplasmosis), metastases (thyroid, melanoma — micronodular type), sarcoidosis. Memory aid: 'Miliary = Millet seeds = Most commonly Mycobacterium TB.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Chest.
questions.conceptTag: Miliary TB uniform tiny nodules CXR
